source: Incus Server - Appendix A - Workstation Setup: Running Incus as a lab on a workstation or notebook

options: Running a lab or semi-permanent OS/apps on a Rocky Linux workstation or notebook -- not a production server
questions.why: It is a workstation/lab setup, outside the production-server chapters.

questions.q: Which storage backend does the workstation setup use in the example, and how does it compare?
options: dir -- simpler but somewhat slower than zfs (use zfs if you can dedicate a disk)
questions.why: The example picks dir (the default offered is btrfs); dir is slower than zfs, which needs a spare disk.

questions.q: Add your user (here 'incusadmin') to the incus-admin group.
questions.why: usermod -a -G incus-admin <user> lets that user run incus without sudo.

questions.q: How do you verify the install worked and your user has privileges?
options: Run `incus list` as your normal user (no sudo) and get the empty container table
questions.why: incus list without sudo returning the table confirms your user's privileges.

questions.q: Which production chapters does the appendix recommend to continue on a workstation?
questions.why: For a workstation lab, images, profiles and snapshots are the relevant chapters.
